schools.summary: Joseph's order to fill the sacks and return the money: 'and he commanded the steward of his house, saying, Fill the men's sacks with food, as much as they can carry, and put every man's money in his sack's mouth' — Joseph ordering the brothers' sacks filled to the full (all they could carry, the generous provision, to his brethren, the kindness mingled with the test to come, vv.2-5); the money again returned in the sacks (as the first time, 42:25) — Joseph's generosity, and part of the stratagem (the silver placed in the sack's mouth, the kindness that would also test them); Joseph's order (the bounty, the returned money, and, to come, the cup, v.2) preparing the final test of the brethren — to prove whether they would forsake Benjamin as they had forsaken Joseph (37:28), or stand by him (the providence working their repentance, the test of the changed hearts) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And he commanded the steward of his house, saying, Fill the men's sacks with food, as much as they can carry, and put every man's money in his sack's mouth
Joseph's order to fill the sacks and return the money
the generous provision and the returned money (42:25)
the stratagem preparing the final test of the brethren (37:28)

schools.summary: Joseph's silver cup placed in Benjamin's sack: 'and put my cup, the silver cup, in the sack's mouth of the youngest, and his corn money. And he did according to the word that Joseph had spoken' — Joseph ordering his own silver cup placed in Benjamin's (the youngest's) sack (the cup that would be the occasion of the test, vv.4-5, 12, the stratagem to detain Benjamin and prove the brethren, the device of the test); the silver cup (Joseph's own, v.5) — the means of the test (the cup planted in Benjamin's sack, the contrivance to bring the brethren back and try their hearts, toward Benjamin, the other son of Rachel); 'he did according to the word that Joseph had spoken' (the steward doing as ordered, the cup planted, the test set, the providence working, the final proof of the brethren, whether they would abandon Benjamin, 37:28, or stand by him) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And put my cup, the silver cup, in the sack's mouth of the youngest, and his corn money. And he did according to the word that Joseph had spoken
Joseph's silver cup placed in Benjamin's sack
the device of the test, the cup planted in the youngest's sack
the test to prove whether the brethren would stand by Benjamin (37:28)

schools.summary: The brethren sent away at morning: 'as soon as the morning was light, the men were sent away, they and their asses' — the brethren, with their corn (and the planted cup, v.2), sent away early in the morning (the departure homeward, the journey begun, toward Canaan, to Jacob, the setting for the pursuit, vv.4-6); the brethren sent home — with their sacks of corn (and Benjamin, the journey begun, the morning departure); 'they and their asses' (the brethren with their laden asses, the corn, the cup, the homeward journey, the providence, the pursuit to come, v.4, the test about to unfold) — Gill.

themes: As soon as the morning was light, the men were sent away, they and their asses
the brethren sent away at morning
the homeward journey begun with the corn and the hidden cup (v.2)
before the pursuit (v.4)

schools.summary: Joseph sending the steward to pursue the brethren: 'and when they were gone out of the city, and not yet far off, Joseph said unto his steward, Up, follow after the men; and when thou dost overtake them, say unto them, Wherefore have ye rewarded evil for good?' — Joseph sending the steward to pursue the brethren (not yet far from the city) and overtake them (the next stage of the test, the cup, v.2, now to be 'discovered,' the contrivance to bring them back, the stratagem in motion); the charge the steward was to bring — 'Wherefore have ye rewarded evil for good?' (the supposed theft of the cup repaying Joseph's kindness with evil, the accusation, the part of the test, to alarm and prove the brethren, the feigned charge); the pursuit and charge (the cup, the accusation) designed to bring the brethren to the test — whether they would stand by Benjamin (the providence working their repentance, for the evil they had done, 37:28, the stratagem of the loving brother) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And when they were gone out of the city... Joseph said unto his steward, Up, follow after the men... Wherefore have ye rewarded evil for good?
Joseph sending the steward to pursue the brethren
the pursuit and feigned charge, the next stage of the test (v.2)
designed to bring the brethren to the test (37:28)

schools.summary: The cup of divination: 'is not this it in which my lord drinketh, and whereby indeed he divineth? ye have done evil in so doing' — the steward charging the brethren with stealing the cup from which Joseph drank and 'whereby he divineth' (the cup of the great ruler, the accusation, the divining cup); the question — did Joseph practice divination, or was this only part of the feigned charge? Two readings: (a) the dominant reading — Joseph did not really practice divination (forbidden, Leviticus 19:26; Deuteronomy 18:10), the steward's words, and Joseph's own, v.15, part of the stratagem and disguise — Joseph speaking as the Egyptians supposed of their great men (the cup-divination, hydromancy, being a known Egyptian art), keeping up the appearance of a heathen lord to conceal himself, the 'divineth' a feigned pretense, or, that Joseph could 'divine,' i.e., discern, things, as by his God-given wisdom, the dreams, ch. 41; (b) a minority reading takes it that the cup was reputed (by the Egyptians) one of divination, the steward speaking of the common Egyptian practice without affirming Joseph used it; the witnesses agreed that Joseph, a worshipper of the true God, did not really use divination (an abomination), but spoke, through the steward, in the character of an Egyptian magnate, part of the disguise and the test); 'ye have done evil in so doing' (the accusation of the theft, the feigned charge to alarm the brethren, the part of the stratagem, the test of their hearts) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

debates.question: Did Joseph really practice divination by the cup?

sides.position: No - Joseph, a worshipper of the true God, did not practice divination (forbidden, Leviticus 19:26; Deuteronomy 18:10); the steward's words were part of the disguise, Joseph speaking in the character of an Egyptian lord (the cup-divination a known Egyptian art) to conceal himself; the 'divineth' a feigned pretense (or his God-given discernment)
The cup was reputed (by the Egyptians) one of divination; the steward spoke of the common Egyptian practice without affirming Joseph actually used it

themes: Is not this it in which my lord drinketh, and whereby indeed he divineth? ye have done evil in so doing
the cup of divination (the crux of Joseph's divining)
Joseph not really practicing divination (forbidden, Leviticus 19:26; Deuteronomy 18:10)
but speaking through the steward in the character of an Egyptian lord - the disguise and the test

schools.summary: The steward overtaking the brethren with the charge: 'and he overtook them, and he spake unto them these same words' — the steward, pursuing (v.4), overtaking the brethren and speaking the charge (vv.4-5, the accusation of stealing the cup, the confrontation, the test advancing, the words delivered); the steward catching up with the brethren (not far from the city, v.4) — the pursuit ended (the brethren stopped, the charge to be laid); 'he spake unto them these same words' (the steward delivering Joseph's charge, vv.4-5, the theft, the divining cup, the accusation laid, the test, the brethren's protest, vv.7-9, to follow, the providence working) — Gill.

themes: And he overtook them, and he spake unto them these same words
the steward overtaking the brethren with the charge
the accusation of the theft laid (vv.4-5)
the test advancing (the brethren's protest, vv.7-9)

schools.summary: The brethren's protest of innocence: 'and they said unto him, Wherefore saith my lord these words? God forbid that thy servants should do according to this thing' — the brethren, astonished at the charge (vv.4-5), protesting — why such words? (conscious of no such theft, the surprise of the innocent, in this matter, the indignant question); the strong denial — 'God forbid' (far be it) that they should do such a thing (steal the cup, the protest of innocence, the abhorrence of the charge, the clear conscience, in this matter, though guilty of Joseph, v.16); the brethren's confident denial (vv.8-9) — sure of their innocence (of the theft, the very confidence that the test would shake, when the cup was found, v.12, the providence, their conscience, guilty of Joseph, 37:28, soon to awaken, v.16) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And they said unto him, Wherefore saith my lord these words? God forbid that thy servants should do according to this thing
the brethren's protest of innocence
the indignant denial of the theft, the clear conscience (in this matter)
the confidence the test would shake (v.12), the conscience yet to awaken (37:28; v.16)

schools.summary: The brethren's appeal to their honesty: 'behold, the money, which we found in our sacks' mouths, we brought again unto thee out of the land of Canaan: how then should we steal out of thy lord's house silver or gold?' — the brethren pleading their honesty: they had brought back the money found in their sacks (42:35; 43:21) — proof that they were no thieves (the argument from their proven honesty, the appeal to their integrity); the brethren having returned the money (43:21) — a proof of their honesty (the argument that they would not steal who had returned what they might have kept); 'how then should we steal out of thy lord's house silver or gold?' (the inference from their honesty, having returned the money, would they steal the cup?, the confident appeal, the protest of innocence, the test that would shake them, v.12, the argument of the, in this, honest men) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Behold, the money, which we found in our sacks' mouths, we brought again unto thee... how then should we steal out of thy lord's house silver or gold?
the brethren's appeal to their honesty
the returned money (43:21) the proof they were no thieves
the confident appeal the test would shake (v.12)

schools.summary: The brethren's rash oath staking their lives: 'with whomsoever of thy servants it be found, both let him die, and we also will be my lord's bondmen' — the brethren, confident of their innocence, rashly offering that the one found with the cup should die, and the rest become bondmen (the rash, over-confident oath, like Jacob's, 31:32, the offer staking their lives and freedom); the rash sentence (death for the thief) — the brethren's over-confidence (sure none had the cup, the rash oath, which the steward would moderate, v.10, the imprudent vow); 'we also will be my lord's bondmen' (the brethren offering their freedom, the confident stake, the test that would find the cup with Benjamin, v.12, the providence, the offer that would bring them to the test of standing by Benjamin, vv.16-17, the rash vow) — JFB, Gill.

themes: With whomsoever of thy servants it be found, both let him die, and we also will be my lord's bondmen
the brethren's rash oath staking their lives
the rash, over-confident vow (like Jacob's, 31:32)
the test that would find the cup with Benjamin (v.12)

schools.summary: The steward moderating the oath: 'and he said, Now also let it be according unto your words: he with whom it is found shall be my servant; and ye shall be blameless' — the steward, accepting the brethren's terms but moderating them: only the one found with the cup should be his servant (bondman), and the rest go free (the softened sentence, not death and all bondmen, but the one a servant, the part of the stratagem, to detain only Benjamin, and test the brethren's response, vv.16-17); only the guilty one (with the cup) should be a servant — the moderated penalty (the stratagem to detain Benjamin, v.12, the cup in his sack, the test set); 'ye shall be blameless' (the rest declared free, the design to single out Benjamin, and prove whether the brethren would abandon him, as they had Joseph, 37:28, or stand by him, the providence working their repentance) — Gill.

themes: And he said, Now also let it be according unto your words: he with whom it is found shall be my servant; and ye shall be blameless
the steward moderating the oath
the softened sentence (only the one found a servant, the rest free)
the design to single out Benjamin and prove the brethren (37:28)

schools.summary: The brethren opening their sacks for the search: 'then they speedily took down every man his sack to the ground, and opened every man his sack' — the brethren, confident of their innocence, quickly taking down and opening their sacks for the search (the eager readiness, sure no cup would be found, the haste of the, in this, innocent, the search begun); the brethren's haste — their confidence (none doubting his own innocence, the eager opening, the readiness for the search); 'opened every man his sack' (each sack opened for the search, the confident readiness, the search that would find the cup with Benjamin, v.12, the providence, the test about to spring) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Then they speedily took down every man his sack to the ground, and opened every man his sack
the brethren opening their sacks for the search
the eager readiness of the (in this) innocent, confident no cup would be found
the search that would find the cup with Benjamin (v.12)

schools.summary: The cup found in Benjamin's sack: 'and he searched, and began at the eldest, and left at the youngest: and the cup was found in Benjamin's sack' — the steward searching the sacks in order, from the eldest (Reuben) to the youngest (Benjamin) (the deliberate search, heightening the suspense, the cup found last, the order that drew out the moment, the search building to its end); the search from eldest to youngest — the suspense (each sack clear, the brethren's confidence rising, until the last, the dramatic order, the cup found at the end, with Benjamin); 'the cup was found in Benjamin's sack' (the cup discovered with Benjamin, planted there, v.2, the test sprung, the brethren's confidence shattered, the crisis, the youngest, Rachel's son, the one Jacob most loved, now charged, the providence bringing them to the test of standing by Benjamin, vv.13-17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he searched, and began at the eldest, and left at the youngest: and the cup was found in Benjamin's sack
the cup found in Benjamin's sack
the deliberate search from eldest to youngest, the suspense rising
the test sprung, the crisis fallen on Rachel's son (vv.13-17)

schools.summary: The brethren rending their clothes in grief: 'then they rent their clothes, and laded every man his ass, and returned to the city' — the brethren, at the finding of the cup (v.12), rending their clothes (the sign of deepest grief and distress, as Jacob, 37:34; Reuben, 37:29, the anguish, the dismay, the loaded asses and the return to the city, to face Joseph); the rending of clothes (the token of great grief) — the brethren's distress (the dismay at the crisis, the grief, and, the witnesses note, the love for Benjamin now shown — they did not abandon him, but all returned with him, the proof of their changed hearts); 'laded every man his ass, and returned to the city' (the brethren all returning with Benjamin, not abandoning him, as they had Joseph, 37:28, the changed hearts, all going back to share his fate, the return to face Joseph, vv.14-17, the providence working their repentance) — Gill.

themes: Then they rent their clothes, and laded every man his ass, and returned to the city
the brethren rending their clothes in grief
the deepest grief and distress (as 37:34)
the changed hearts - all returning with Benjamin, not abandoning him (37:28)

schools.summary: The brethren falling before Joseph: 'and Judah and his brethren came to Joseph's house; for he was yet there: and they fell before him on the ground' — the brethren (Judah named first, the leader, the speaker, vv.16-34) coming to Joseph's house and falling prostrate before him (the deep submission, the suppliants before the governor, the bowing, a further fulfillment of Joseph's dreams, 37:7-9, the prostration of the distressed); Judah named first — the spokesman and leader (who would make the great plea, vv.18-34, the foremost of the brethren, the one who had pledged surety for Benjamin, 43:9); 'they fell before him on the ground' (the brethren prostrate before Joseph, the dreams of the bowing sheaves and stars, 37:7-9, again fulfilled, the deep submission, the providence, the suppliants before the brother they did not know) — Gill.

themes: And Judah and his brethren came to Joseph's house; for he was yet there: and they fell before him on the ground
the brethren falling before Joseph
the deep submission, the dreams again fulfilled (37:7-9)
Judah the leader who would plead (43:9; vv.18-34)

schools.summary: Joseph charging the brethren and keeping up the disguise: 'and Joseph said unto them, What deed is this that ye have done? wot ye not that such a man as I can certainly divine?' — Joseph charging the brethren with the supposed theft of the cup (continuing the test, the feigned indignation, the part of the stratagem, the charge laid); Joseph saying 'such a man as I can certainly divine' — Joseph keeping up the appearance of an Egyptian magnate who could divine (discern hidden things), as the steward had said (v.5, the disguise maintained, lest he be known, the witnesses noting, as at v.5, that Joseph did not really practice the forbidden divination, Leviticus 19:26, but spoke in the Egyptian character, or of his God-given discernment, by which he had read the dreams, ch. 41 — the feigned claim part of the test and the concealment); Joseph's charge and claim (the disguise) advancing the test — to alarm the brethren and prove their hearts (toward Benjamin, the providence working their repentance, for the deed against Joseph, 37:28, which their consciences would now own, v.16, the stratagem of the loving brother) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB.

themes: And Joseph said unto them, What deed is this that ye have done? wot ye not that such a man as I can certainly divine?
Joseph charging the brethren and keeping up the disguise
Joseph in the Egyptian character (not really divining, Leviticus 19:26; v.5)
the charge advancing the test of their hearts (37:28; v.16)

schools.summary: Judah's confession - 'God hath found out the iniquity': 'and Judah said, What shall we say unto my lord? what shall we speak? or how shall we clear ourselves? God hath found out the iniquity of thy servants: behold, we are my lord's servants, both we, and he also with whom the cup is found' — Judah, unable to explain the cup (knowing not how it came to Benjamin's sack), confessing they could not clear themselves (the helplessness before the charge, the speechlessness of the, in this matter innocent, yet, guilty conscience); Judah's great word — 'God hath found out the iniquity of thy servants': Judah owning that God was now reckoning with them — not chiefly for the cup (of which they were innocent), but for their old, hidden iniquity (the sin against Joseph, 37:28, long unrepented, the awakened conscience, as at 42:21, 'we are verily guilty concerning our brother,' the recognition of God's righteous hand finding out their sin, Numbers 32:23, 'be sure your sin will find you out,' the providence bringing the buried guilt to light, the confession of the guilty conscience, that God is just, and was now visiting their old crime, the deep work of repentance); 'behold, we are my lord's servants, both we, and he also with whom the cup is found' (Judah offering that all, not Benjamin only, should be bondmen, the brethren standing by Benjamin, not abandoning him, 37:28, the changed hearts, all sharing his fate, the submission to God's hand) — Clarke, Wesley, Calvin, Gill.

debates.question: What iniquity does Judah confess God has 'found out'?

sides.position: Not chiefly the cup (of which they were innocent), but their old, hidden sin against Joseph (37:28; 42:21) - the buried guilt found out (Numbers 32:23), the conscience awakened, God owned as righteous; the deep work of repentance

themes: And Judah said... how shall we clear ourselves? God hath found out the iniquity of thy servants: behold, we are my lord's servants
Judah's confession - 'God hath found out the iniquity' (the crux of the awakened conscience)
God reckoning for their old sin against Joseph (37:28; 42:21), not the cup - the buried guilt found out (Numbers 32:23)
the changed hearts standing by Benjamin (all offering to be bondmen)

schools.summary: Joseph's sentence detaining only Benjamin: 'and he said, God forbid that I should do so: but the man in whose hand the cup is found, he shall be my servant; and as for you, get you up in peace unto your father' — Joseph refusing to make all the brethren bondmen (as Judah offered, v.16), only the one with the cup (Benjamin) to be his servant (the part of the test, to single out Benjamin, and see if the brethren would abandon him); the sentence — only Benjamin (with the cup) to be detained as servant, the rest free (the test sharpened, would the brethren go home in peace, leaving Benjamin, as they had left Joseph, 37:28?); 'get you up in peace unto your father' (Joseph bidding the rest go home free, the very test — would they abandon Benjamin and go up to their father, as they had abandoned Joseph?, the providence proving their hearts, and drawing out Judah's plea, vv.18-34, the crisis) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he said, God forbid that I should do so: but the man in whose hand the cup is found, he shall be my servant; and as for you, get you up in peace unto your father
Joseph's sentence detaining only Benjamin
only Benjamin detained, the rest sent home free - the test sharpened
whether the brethren would abandon Benjamin as they had Joseph (37:28), drawing out Judah's plea (vv.18-34)

schools.summary: The beginning of Judah's great plea: 'then Judah came near unto him, and said, Oh my lord, let thy servant, I pray thee, speak a word in my lord's ears, and let not thine anger burn against thy servant: for thou art even as Pharaoh' — Judah drawing near and beginning the great speech (the longest in Genesis, vv.18-34) — the moving, earnest plea for Benjamin (the intercession of the surety, 43:9, the approach of the suppliant, the beginning of one of Scripture's most eloquent and touching pleas); Judah's intercession — humble ('let thy servant speak,' 'let not thine anger burn'), reverent ('thou art even as Pharaoh,' owning Joseph's power), earnest, and self-forgetting (pleading for Benjamin and the father, vv.20-34, the masterpiece of natural eloquence, so moving that it broke Joseph down, 45:1-2, the plea of the changed Judah — the very man who had said 'let us sell him,' 37:26-27, now pleading for the other son of Rachel, and offering himself in his stead, v.33, the transformation of Judah, the intercession that proved the brethren's hearts changed); 'thou art even as Pharaoh' (Judah owning Joseph's great power, as Pharaoh, the supreme ruler, the humble reverence, the suppliant's submission, the beginning of the plea, the intercession that would move Joseph to reveal himself, 45:1) — Henry, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

debates.question: What is the character of Judah's great plea?

sides.position: The moving, self-forgetting intercession of the changed man who had once sold Joseph (37:26-27), now pleading for Benjamin and offering himself in his stead (v.33) - the masterpiece of eloquence that broke Joseph down (45:1-2), the proof of the brethren's transformed hearts

themes: Then Judah came near unto him, and said, Oh my lord, let thy servant, I pray thee, speak a word in my lord's ears... for thou art even as Pharaoh
the beginning of Judah's great plea (the crux of the intercession)
the longest, most eloquent and touching speech in Genesis (vv.18-34)
the changed man who had sold Joseph (37:26-27) now pleading for Benjamin, offering himself (v.33); it broke Joseph down (45:1-2)

schools.summary: Judah rehearsing Joseph's question about their family: 'my lord asked his servants, saying, Have ye a father, or a brother?' — Judah, in his plea, rehearsing how Joseph (the governor) had first asked them of their father and brother (42:7-13, the recounting of the history, to move Joseph, the beginning of the narrative, vv.19-29, leading to the plea, vv.30-34); Joseph's earlier question (42:7-13) — about their father (Jacob) and brother (Benjamin, the inquiry that began the matter, rehearsed by Judah); Judah's recounting (vv.19-29) — the skillful, moving narrative (to show how the demand for Benjamin had bound the father's heart, the building of the plea, the intercession for Benjamin, and the father, vv.30-34) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: My lord asked his servants, saying, Have ye a father, or a brother?
Judah rehearsing Joseph's question about their family
the recounting of the history (42:7-13) to move Joseph
the building of the plea (vv.30-34)

schools.summary: Judah recounting the father's love for Benjamin: 'and we said unto my lord, We have a father, an old man, and a child of his old age, a little one; and his brother is dead, and he alone is left of his mother, and his father loveth him' — Judah rehearsing their answer (42:13): an aged father (Jacob), and a youngest son (Benjamin), the child of his old age, the only one left of his mother (Rachel, the other, Joseph, 'dead'), and dearly loved by the father (the recounting that pressed the father's love for Benjamin, the plea building); the reference — Joseph ('dead,' as the brethren supposed, or said, 37:33), Benjamin the only one left of Rachel (the special love of the father, for the last of Rachel's sons); 'his father loveth him' (the father's special love for Benjamin, the child of his old age, the last of Rachel, the love that bound Jacob's life to the lad, v.30, the ground of the plea, the father would die if Benjamin returned not, vv.30-31, the intercession building) — Gill.

themes: And we said unto my lord, We have a father, an old man, and a child of his old age... and his father loveth him
Judah recounting the father's love for Benjamin
the aged father, the child of his old age, the last of Rachel (42:13; 37:33)
the father's love, the ground of the plea (the life bound to the lad, vv.30-31)

schools.summary: Judah recounting Joseph's demand for Benjamin: 'and thou saidst unto thy servants, Bring him down unto me, that I may set mine eyes upon him' — Judah rehearsing Joseph's demand (42:15, 20) that Benjamin be brought down to Egypt (to 'set eyes upon him,' to see him, the demand that had forced the bringing of Benjamin, the recounting, to show the necessity that had bound the father, the plea building); Joseph's requirement (42:20) — that Benjamin be brought (the condition of their return, the demand rehearsed); 'that I may set mine eyes upon him' (Joseph's expressed wish to see Benjamin, his own brother, the other son of Rachel, whom he longed to see, the demand, in truth Joseph's love, the recounting building the plea, the demand that had cost the father so dearly, vv.30-31) — Gill.

themes: And thou saidst unto thy servants, Bring him down unto me, that I may set mine eyes upon him
Judah recounting Joseph's demand for Benjamin
the demand (42:15, 20) that forced the bringing of Benjamin
in truth Joseph's love, the plea building (vv.30-31)

schools.summary: Judah recounting their warning about the father: 'and we said unto my lord, The lad cannot leave his father: for if he should leave his father, his father would die' — Judah rehearsing their earlier warning (42:38; 44:31): that Benjamin could not leave Jacob without the father's death (so bound was Jacob's life to the lad, the warning that pressed the danger, the recounting building the plea, the father's life at stake); the father's bond to Benjamin — Jacob could not part with him (42:38, the lad the prop of the father's life, the bond pressed in the plea); 'his father would die' (the warning that Jacob would die if Benjamin were lost, v.31, the danger pressed, the ground of the plea, the father's life bound to the lad, vv.30-31, the intercession building) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And we said unto my lord, The lad cannot leave his father: for if he should leave his father, his father would die
Judah recounting their warning about the father
the father's life bound to the lad (42:38)
the ground of the plea (the father would die, v.31)

schools.summary: Judah recounting Joseph's condition: 'and thou saidst unto thy servants, Except your youngest brother come down with you, ye shall see my face no more' — Judah rehearsing Joseph's condition (42:15-20; 43:3, 5): that they should not see his face (buy more corn) unless Benjamin came (the condition that forced the bringing of Benjamin, the recounting building the plea, the necessity laid on the father); the condition (43:3) — Benjamin must come (the requirement that bound them, the demand rehearsed); 'ye shall see my face no more' (the threat, no more corn, no more audience, without Benjamin, the necessity that forced the father to send the lad, 43:8-14, the recounting building the plea, the demand that had cost the father, vv.30-34) — Gill.

themes: And thou saidst unto thy servants, Except your youngest brother come down with you, ye shall see my face no more
Judah recounting Joseph's condition
the condition (42:20; 43:3) that forced the bringing of Benjamin
the necessity laid on the father (43:8-14), the plea building (vv.30-34)

schools.summary: Judah recounting their report to the father: 'and it came to pass when we came up unto thy servant my father, we told him the words of my lord' — Judah rehearsing how, returning to Jacob (after the first journey, ch. 42), they had told him Joseph's words (the demand for Benjamin, the report to the father, the recounting, to show how the demand had distressed the father, the plea building); the return to Jacob (42:29) — the report of the journey (the bringing of Joseph's words to the father); 'we told him the words of my lord' (the brethren's report of Joseph's demand, 42:29-34, the words that grieved the father, the recounting building the plea, the demand that bound the father, vv.30-34) — Gill.

themes: And it came to pass when we came up unto thy servant my father, we told him the words of my lord
Judah recounting their report to the father
the return to Jacob (42:29) and the report of Joseph's demand
the words that grieved the father, the plea building (vv.30-34)

schools.summary: The father's bidding to buy more food: 'and our father said, Go again, and buy us a little food' — Judah rehearsing how Jacob, when the corn was spent (43:2), bade them go again to Egypt to buy a little food (the father's bidding, that occasioned the second journey, and the debate over Benjamin, 43:2-10, the recounting building the plea); Jacob's bidding (43:2) — the second journey to buy corn (the necessity, the famine, the bidding that raised again the matter of Benjamin); 'buy us a little food' (the need that drove the second journey, the famine, 43:1, the bidding that forced the bringing of Benjamin, Judah's surety, 43:8-9, the recounting building the plea, vv.30-34) — Gill.

themes: And our father said, Go again, and buy us a little food
the father's bidding to buy more food
the second journey (43:2) driven by the famine (43:1)
the bidding that forced the bringing of Benjamin (43:8-9)

schools.summary: Judah recounting the condition of Benjamin's coming: 'and we said, We cannot go down: if our youngest brother be with us, then will we go down: for we may not see the man's face, except our youngest brother be with us' — Judah rehearsing their answer to Jacob (43:3-5): they could not go down (to buy corn) without Benjamin (Joseph's condition, v.23, the necessity that forced the bringing of the lad, the recounting building the plea); the condition (43:4-5) — only with Benjamin could they go (the requirement laid on the father, the necessity rehearsed); 'we may not see the man's face, except our youngest brother be with us' (Joseph's condition, v.23, rehearsed, the bind that forced Jacob to send Benjamin, 43:11-14, the recounting building the plea, the demand that bound the father, vv.30-34) — Gill.

themes: And we said, We cannot go down: if our youngest brother be with us, then will we go down...
Judah recounting the condition of Benjamin's coming
the necessity (43:3-5; v.23) that forced the father to send the lad
the plea building (vv.30-34)

schools.summary: Judah recounting the father's words about Rachel's two sons: 'and thy servant my father said unto us, Ye know that my wife bare me two sons' — Judah rehearsing Jacob's words: that his wife (Rachel, the beloved wife, called 'my wife' specially) bore him two sons (Joseph and Benjamin, the father's special tie to Rachel's two sons, the recounting that pressed the father's love, for the last of Rachel, vv.28-29, the plea building); 'my wife' (Rachel, the beloved, 29:18, 30) — the two sons (Joseph and Benjamin) of the favored wife (the special love, the tie pressed in the plea); Jacob's words (of Rachel's two sons) — the ground of the father's special love for Benjamin (the last of Rachel, Joseph being 'dead,' the recounting building the plea, the father's life bound to the lad, vv.30-31) — Gill.

themes: And thy servant my father said unto us, Ye know that my wife bare me two sons
Judah recounting the father's words about Rachel's two sons
the special love for Rachel's sons (29:18, 30)
the ground of the plea (the father bound to the last of Rachel, vv.30-31)

schools.summary: Judah recounting the father's grief over Joseph: 'and the one went out from me, and I said, Surely he is torn in pieces; and I saw him not since' — Judah rehearsing Jacob's words of Joseph (the one of Rachel's two who 'went out' and was lost): that he was 'torn in pieces' (the brethren's lie, 37:33, 'an evil beast hath devoured him') and seen no more (the father's grief over the supposed dead Joseph, the recounting, that pressed how a like loss of Benjamin would kill the father, the plea building); Jacob's belief (the brethren's deception, 37:33) — Joseph torn by a beast (the father's unhealed grief, the loss pressed in the plea, the irony — Judah recounting the very grief he had caused, 37:26-28); 'I saw him not since' (Jacob's grief over Joseph, unseen since, supposed dead, the recounting, Judah, who had sold Joseph, 37:28, now feeling the father's grief, the changed heart, the plea building, the danger of a like loss of Benjamin, v.29) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the one went out from me, and I said, Surely he is torn in pieces; and I saw him not since
Judah recounting the father's grief over Joseph
Jacob's grief (the brethren's deception, 37:33)
the irony - Judah recounting the very grief he had caused (37:26-28), the changed heart now feeling it

schools.summary: Judah recounting the father's fear for Benjamin: 'and if ye take this also from me, and mischief befall him, ye shall bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to the grave' — Judah rehearsing Jacob's words (42:38): if Benjamin too were taken and harmed, the father's gray hairs would go down with sorrow to the grave (the father would die of grief, the fear that bound the father to the lad, the recounting, the heart of the plea — the father's life at stake, the danger pressed); Jacob's fear (42:38) — that Benjamin, the last of Rachel, would be lost like Joseph (the danger, the father's dread); 'ye shall bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to the grave' (the father's death foreseen, if Benjamin were lost, 42:38; v.31, the heart of the plea, the father's life bound to the lad, the danger that moved Judah's offer, v.33, the intercession at its height) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And if ye take this also from me, and mischief befall him, ye shall bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to the grave
Judah recounting the father's fear for Benjamin
the father's death foreseen if Benjamin were lost (42:38; v.31)
the danger that moved Judah's offer (v.33)

schools.summary: Judah pressing the father's life bound to Benjamin: 'now therefore when I come to thy servant my father, and the lad be not with us; seeing that his life is bound up in the lad's life' — Judah pressing the heart of the matter: that Jacob's very life was 'bound up' in Benjamin's life (so dear was the lad, the last of Rachel, if Benjamin returned not, the father would die, v.31, the bond that was the ground of the plea, the intercession at its height); the tender phrase ('his life is bound up in the lad's life') — the father's life so tied to Benjamin that the loss would kill him (the depth of the father's love, the bond pressed in the plea); Judah's pressing the bond (the father's life in the lad's) — the ground of his offer (v.33, to be bondman in Benjamin's stead, lest the father die, the heart of the intercession, the love for the father, and Benjamin, moving the plea) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: Now therefore when I come to thy servant my father, and the lad be not with us; seeing that his life is bound up in the lad's life
Judah pressing the father's life bound to Benjamin
the father's life so tied to Benjamin that the loss would kill him
the ground of Judah's offer (v.33)

schools.summary: Judah foreseeing the father's death if Benjamin returned not: 'it shall come to pass, when he seeth that the lad is not with us, that he will die: and thy servants shall bring down the gray hairs of thy servant our father with sorrow to the grave' — Judah foreseeing that Jacob, seeing Benjamin not returned, would die of grief (the father's death, if the lad were lost, v.29, the brethren bringing down the father's gray hairs with sorrow to the grave, the very thing they dreaded, the heart of the plea, the father's life at stake); the certain death of the father (of grief) if Benjamin returned not — the danger (the love that bound Jacob's life to the lad, the heart of the intercession); 'bring down the gray hairs of thy servant our father with sorrow to the grave' (the brethren the cause of the father's death, if Benjamin were lost, the dread that moved Judah's offer, v.33, the heart of the plea, the love for the father) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: It shall come to pass, when he seeth that the lad is not with us, that he will die...
Judah foreseeing the father's death if Benjamin returned not
the father's death foreseen of grief (v.29)
the dread that moved Judah's offer (v.33)

schools.summary: Judah recalling his surety for Benjamin: 'for thy servant became surety for the lad unto my father, saying, If I bring him not unto thee, then I shall bear the blame to my father for ever' — Judah recalling his pledge (43:8-9): he had become surety for Benjamin, undertaking to bear the blame for ever if he brought him not back (the personal pledge that bound Judah, the ground of his offer, v.33, to take Benjamin's place, the surety's obligation); Judah's suretyship (43:9) — the pledge to answer for Benjamin (the personal bond, the obligation that moved his offer, the surety standing for the lad, a figure, the fathers note, of Christ the surety, Hebrews 7:22); 'I shall bear the blame to my father for ever' (Judah's pledge to bear the blame, the bond that bound him to bring Benjamin back, or take his place, the ground of his offer, v.33, the surety's faithfulness) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: For thy servant became surety for the lad unto my father, saying, If I bring him not unto thee, then I shall bear the blame to my father for ever
Judah recalling his surety for Benjamin
the personal pledge (43:9) that bound Judah
the surety standing for the lad (a figure of Christ the surety, Hebrews 7:22), moving his offer (v.33)

schools.summary: Judah's offer to be a bondman in Benjamin's stead: 'now therefore, I pray thee, let thy servant abide instead of the lad a bondman to my lord; and let the lad go up with his brethren' — Judah's great offer: Judah offering himself in Benjamin's place — to remain a bondman (a slave) to Joseph, that Benjamin might go free to his father (the noble substitution, the self-sacrifice of the surety, 43:9, the transformation of Judah, the very man who had sold Joseph into slavery, 37:26-28, now offering himself into slavery to save the other son of Rachel, the love for the father and Benjamin that gave himself, the climax of the plea); Judah's offer to be a bondman in the lad's stead a striking figure of Christ — Judah, the head of the tribe from which Christ came (49:10; Matthew 1:2-3), offering himself a surety and substitute for his brother (Hebrews 7:22, 'Jesus made a surety of a better testament,' the type of the Redeemer who took the place of his brethren, becoming a servant, Philippians 2:7, and bearing their blame, that they might go free, the substitute who gave himself, the foreshadowing of the great Substitution, Isaiah 53:6; 2 Corinthians 5:21, 'made him to be sin for us,' the surety of God's Benjamins, standing in their stead); 'let the lad go up with his brethren' (Judah's care that Benjamin go free to the father, the self-forgetting love, the offer that proved the brethren's hearts changed, no longer the men who sold their brother, but one offering himself for him, the plea that broke Joseph down, 45:1) — Calvin, Henry, Gill.

debates.question: What does Judah's offer to be a bondman in Benjamin's stead display?

sides.position: The noble self-sacrifice of the surety (43:9) and the transformation of Judah - the man who had sold Joseph into slavery (37:26-28) now offering himself into slavery to save the other son of Rachel
A striking type of Christ, the surety and substitute (Hebrews 7:22), who took his brethren's place and became a servant (Philippians 2:7; Isaiah 53:6; 2 Corinthians 5:21) that they might go free

themes: Now therefore, I pray thee, let thy servant abide instead of the lad a bondman to my lord; and let the lad go up with his brethren
Judah's offer to be a bondman in Benjamin's stead (the crux of the substitution)
the man who sold Joseph (37:26-28) now offering himself a bondman to save Benjamin - the surety's self-sacrifice (43:9)
a type of Christ the surety and substitute (Hebrews 7:22; Isaiah 53:6; Philippians 2:7)

schools.summary: Judah's closing plea - he cannot face the father without Benjamin: 'for how shall I go up to my father, and the lad be not with me? lest peradventure I see the evil that shall come on my father' — Judah closing his plea with the unanswerable appeal: he could not go up to his father without Benjamin (could not bear to face Jacob and tell him the lad was lost, the heart-cry of the changed man, the love for the father that would rather be a bondman, v.33, than bring such grief, the climax of the intercession); Judah's dread — he could not bear to 'see the evil' (the grief, the death, v.31) that would come on Jacob if Benjamin returned not (the horror of the father's death, the love that recoiled from being the cause of it, as he had been of the grief over Joseph, 37:28, the self-forgetting plea); Judah's plea — the noblest of pleas, the changed man pleading for his brother and father, offering himself (v.33) — so moving Joseph that he could no longer refrain, and revealed himself (45:1, 'I am Joseph,' the intercession that won the day, the proof of the brethren's repentance, the deep work of God in them, from the cruelty of ch. 37 to this self-sacrificing love, the climax of the long testing) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: For how shall I go up to my father, and the lad be not with me? lest peradventure I see the evil that shall come on my father
Judah's closing plea - he cannot face the father without Benjamin
the self-forgetting love that would rather be a bondman (v.33) than grieve the father (v.31; 37:28)
the plea that broke Joseph down and won the day (45:1)
